title: Quiz: Limitations of Classical Computing
description: Test your understanding of the fundamental barriers classical computers face

questions.question: What physical phenomenon causes problems for transistors below ~5nm?
questions.explanation: Quantum tunneling allows electrons to 'tunnel' through barriers they classically couldn't cross. Below ~5nm, electrons tunnel through transistor gates, causing leakage and unreliable switching.

questions.question: The Halting Problem is an example of what type of problem?
questions.explanation: The Halting Problem is undecidable - no algorithm can determine for ALL programs whether they will halt or run forever. This was proven by Alan Turing in 1936.

questions.question: According to Amdahl's Law, with infinite processors and 95% parallelizable code, you can achieve at most 20x speedup.
questions.explanation: True! With 95% parallel code (5% sequential), the maximum speedup is 1/(0.05) = 20x, no matter how many processors you add. The sequential portion becomes the bottleneck.

questions.question: How much memory is needed to classically simulate a 50-qubit quantum system?
questions.explanation: 50 qubits require 2^50 ≈ 10^15 complex amplitudes. At 16 bytes each, that's about 16 petabytes - beyond most supercomputers!

questions.question: Landauer's principle sets a minimum energy for:
questions.explanation: Landauer's principle states that erasing one bit of information requires minimum energy kT·ln(2), about 3×10^-21 joules at room temperature.

questions.question: Why is the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) considered intractable?
questions.explanation: TSP brute force requires checking all n! possible routes, which grows faster than exponentially. For 30 cities, that's about 10^32 permutations!

questions.question: Quantum computers can solve undecidable problems that classical computers cannot.
questions.explanation: False! Quantum computers are still Turing-equivalent - they can compute exactly the same set of problems as classical computers. Undecidable problems remain undecidable for quantum computers too.

questions.question: The 'quantum supremacy' threshold is approximately:
questions.explanation: Around 50 qubits, simulating a quantum computer classically requires petabytes of memory and astronomical time, making quantum computation faster even with noisy qubits.
